title: Existing app takeover | Bc. Ondřej Halata (halatao.cz)

seo.title: Existing app takeover | Bc. Ondřej Halata (halatao.cz)
seo.description: I take over existing or partially delivered web applications, reduce delivery risk, stabilise the stack, and create a realistic path for ongoing improvement.

page.title: Existing app takeover | Bc. Ondřej Halata (halatao.cz)
page.h1: Existing app takeover and structured improvement
page.description: I take over existing or partially delivered web applications, reduce delivery risk, stabilise the stack, and create a realistic path for ongoing improvement.

hero.title: Take over the app without betting the business on a rewrite
hero.subtitle: Useful when a supplier changed, a product is stuck, or the codebase works just well enough to be risky to touch.

intro: Many teams need help after a vendor handover, an unfinished delivery, or a period where the application kept moving without strong technical ownership.
The hardest part is rarely the code alone. It is the uncertainty around what can change safely and what might break operations.
That is why the first step is not a dramatic rebuild. The right first move is to map dependencies, release flow, operational risk, and the real business pressure around the system.

sections.title: Where this service is the right fit
body: This service is valuable when the system is already important to the business but future delivery feels risky, opaque, or overly dependent on inherited knowledge.
bullets: unfinished delivery with no technical lead
running app with unclear operational risk
product roadmap blocked by technical uncertainty

sections.title: What I typically handle and deliver
body: The first phase focuses on codebase orientation, deployment review, risk mapping, and a prioritised plan grounded in business impact rather than technical aesthetics.

sections.title: How the work is run
body: Once the system is mapped, we can define what needs immediate protection, what should be improved incrementally, and whether any bigger rewrite is actually justified.

sections.title: What the engagement should achieve
body: The main outcome is decision confidence: you understand the risk, the practical next steps, and the technical cost of different delivery paths.
bullets: faster onboarding into the inherited stack
safer basis for ongoing product work

faq.question: Can you take over an app with little or no documentation?
faq.answer: Yes. That is common. It requires a structured discovery phase based on the codebase, infrastructure, release flow, and people who know the business process.

faq.question: Does an older stack automatically mean we should rebuild?
faq.answer: No. The right question is what concrete risk or delivery friction the current stack creates. Age alone is not a business case for a rewrite.

faq.question: Can you continue with ongoing development after the audit?
faq.answer: Yes. I can stay on for stabilisation and delivery, or support an internal team with architecture, implementation, and technical direction.

faq.question: Can you work alongside internal staff?
faq.answer: Yes. Existing-app takeovers work best when technical discovery and internal process knowledge are combined.

for: running applications after a vendor or team change
unfinished products that need structure and technical ownership
systems that need stabilisation before bigger roadmap work
notFor: projects with no access to infrastructure or source code
pure visual redesigns without technical ownership
rewrite-first mandates with no discovery phase

cta.label: Discuss your project
cta.note: Share the business context, expected outcome, and current constraints. I will tell you whether the project is a fit.
